text:
# Page 1^page=1
The EU Digital Product Passport for Batteries – Webinar 2 Latest Updates, Key Requirements and Industry Perspectives DG GROW – European Commission July 7, 2026

# Page 2^page=2
Welcome & Housekeeping Rules for Participants • Microphones are muted by default. • Participants should remain muted unless invited to speak. • Asking Questions: • After each session slot, there will be 10 minutes dedicated to questions relevant to that section. • Participants are invited to raise their hand; the moderator will give the floor in order of request (priority-based). • Alternatively, questions can be submitted via the Q&A chat and may be addressed at the end of the session during the dedicated live Q&A. • Not all questions may be answered live due to time constraints; unanswered questions will be addressed later. • Message Guidelines: • Keep messages concise and relevant. • Use the chat only for technical issues (audio, access, connection). • Use the Q&A for content-related questions. • Permissions: • Participants can view the session and use the chat and Q&A features. • Recording Notice: • The session will be recorded. By joining, participants agree to the recording and to the use of their image and voice for documentation purposes.

# Page 3^page=3
Agenda 10:00 – 10:10 (CEST) Introduction 10:10 – 10:20 (CEST) Opening remarks Stefka DZHUMALIEVA 10:20 – 10:30 (CEST) Participant Survey: Discussing main challenges and industry perspectives Maria VASCONCELOS 10:30 – 10:40 (CEST) Digital Product Passport: Recent developments and upcoming implementation milestones Alexandru ION 10:40 – 10:55 (CEST) Digital Product Passport for Batteries: Updates on Responsibilities and Data Requirements Ewout DEURWAARDER 10:55 – 11:10 (CEST) Q&A on specific presentations 11:10 – 11:20 (CEST) BREAK 11:20 – 11:55 (CEST) Q&A follow up from previous webinar 11:50 – 12:00 (CEST) Conclusions & wrap-up Stefka DZHUMALIEVA

Introduction1

# Page 6^page=6
Maria VASCONCELOS Participant Survey: Discussing main challenges3

# Page 7^page=7
Respondent Profile • 91 participants answered the survey • Battery segments led by industrial batteries (56%) and EVs (47%) • Most respondents are large enterprises (63%) • 56% expect to act as economic operators placing batteries on the EU market, followed by upstream data providers with 25%

# Page 8^page=8
Readiness is progressing 58% 54% 49% 41% 41% 34% 15% 14% Data collection and mapping Supplier engagement IT development Compliance assessment Internal governance and responsibility allocation Pilot projects or testing No activities started yet Other Which implementation activities have already started within your organisation? 12% 15% 34% 29% 10% How would you assess your organization's level of preparation for the Digital Product Passport implementation? Very advanced Advanced Moderate Early stage Not yet started

# Page 9^page=9
Implementation Challenges 63% 59% 58% 53% 39% 17% Data availability and quality Roles and responsibilities across the value chain Dynamic data and lifecycle management Technical implementation and interoperability Supply chain complexity Other What is currently your organisation's biggest implementation challenge?

# Page 10^page=10
Key areas of technical effort 51% 47% 47% 44% 34% 27% 22% 15% 7% Supplier data integration Data collection System integration with existing business processes Data quality management APIs and interoperability Semantic requirements Data hosting arrangements QR codes and unique identifiers Other Which aspects of technical implementation would require the most effort?

# Page 11^page=11
Future Guidance 59% 49% 49% 46% 44% 44% 41% 37% 37% 24% 5% Access rights and confidentiality Dynamic data and update obligations Technical standards and interoperability Practical implementation examples Lifecycle events and management scenarios (e.g. repair, repurposing, remanufacturing, recycling) Data quality requirements Roles and responsibilities across the value chain Registry functionality Relationship with other legislation Delegation of responsibilities Other Which topics would you like to see prioritised in future technical webinars and guidance materials? In terms of lifecycle guidance, respondents pointed most strongly to: • Placing the product on the market (37%) • Manufacturing (20%)

# Page 12^page=12
Timeline readiness 15% 20% 24% 36% 5% How confident are you that the necessary Battery Passport data will be available within your value chain by February 2027? Very confident Somewhat confident Neutral Not very confident Not confident at all

# Page 13^page=13
Alexandru ION Digital Product Passport: Recent Developments4

# Page 14^page=14
Structure of the DPP Registry DPP registry • Website – secure user interface • API – registering the digital product passport and receiving information from the Registry • Verification platform – confirm and verify the completeness and existence of the DPP • A scheme for generating unique registration identifiers • List of verified economic operators • Semantic repository • Log system • Identification and authorisation schemes for registry users. • Storage

# Page 15^page=15
Economic operator provides evidence of identity by the means of one of the following: • a qualified electronic signature (QES) in accordance with eIDAS Regulation • an electronic identification that meets the assurance level ‘high’ in accordance with eIDAS Regulation (available for natural persons acting as sole traders required to be established in the EU) • an electronic attestation of attributes issued under Union law Commission verifies the above information and provides the “verified economic operator” status. After this the EO can register DPP. Economic Operator verification process Economic Operator

# Page 16^page=16
Responsibilities of verified economic operators ❑ Providing all necessary information to complete the registration process ❑ Ensure that information stored in the registry of the DPP is kept accurate, complete and up to date all the times; ❑ Implement appropriate technical and organisational security measures to its IT systems and credentials ❑ Economic operators are responsible for the data submitted and are considered the controlled of that data ❑ Where a verified economic operator authorises a third party to perform registration actions in the registry on its behalf, the verified economic operator will remain fully responsible for compliance

# Page 17^page=17
Value chain actor provides evidence of identity by the means of one of the following: • a qualified electronic signature (QES) in accordance with eIDAS Regulation • an electronic identification that meets the assurance level ‘high’ in accordance with eIDAS Regulation (available for natural persons acting as sole traders required to be established in the EU) • an electronic attestation of attributes issued under Union law Commission verifies the above information and provides the “verified value chain actor” status. After this the Value chain actor can carry out actions in the Registry. Value chain actor verification process Value Chain Actor

# Page 18^page=18
The status of “verified economic operator or supply chain actor” is not permanent. It is retained until: • The electronic identification means used for the verification expire, or • After a maximum of three years from the date of the verification. Once the status expires, the economic operator shall successfully repeat the verification process to regain the verified status. The verification process and status for value chain actors is the same as the one for EO. «Verified» status Economic Operator Value Chain Actor

# Page 19^page=19
They may delegate access rights to third party users acting on their behalf. Personal data entered as part of the user’s profile shall be processed according to Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. They shall be responsible for managing their electronic verification process. In case of relevant changes, they shall be responsible for keeping the data updated Management of user profile data for verified EOs and value chain actors

# Page 20^page=20
DPP Registration Economic Operators register required DPP data via the platform or through an API. Register Verifies DPP Registry Declaration of Conformity DPP restricted data Unique Product Identifier DPP public data Other restricted certificatesThe registry ensures high quality data upon registration through semantic completeness and reference lists (Authorised Representatives, Representation mandates, etc.) DPP data The DPP has to follow the structure and data for which there is a legal requirement to be verified are validated. This elements are provided by the Semantic Repository. The Authorized Representative data is included in the DPP with a legal requirement to be verified against the Authorized Representative repository in the DPP Registry

# Page 21^page=21
Data models and semantic repository for the DPP Semantic repository of the DPP REGISTRY ESPR legal requirements Cross-sectoral standards, semantic attributes, data points Data Models Semantic Assets Data Models Semantic Assets Batteries Sector-specific standards, semantic attributes, data points Examples: data carrier, unique identifier Examples: An Economic Operator placing a battery on the EU market uses the DPP Registry semantic repository to access the required data model and semantic assets, ensuring compliance with DPP rules. DPP

# Page 22^page=22
The Registry performs limited, formal checks: o Verification of mandatory fields (as defined by sectoral legislation) o Confirmation of correct data formatting (structural compliance) Treatment of data No substantive validation of DPP content (responsibility of sectoral laws/enforcement authorities) or storage of DPP content or restricted data.!

# Page 23^page=23
• Helpdesk service operating from 8:00 to 20:00 CET • Automated technical support tool accessible 24-hour basis • Written exchanges to be stored for six months • Commission will provide users guides and other helpingresources Technical Support for the DPP Registry

# Page 24^page=24
Data requirements - if applicable Code EV* LMT​* IND* 1a, 1c if available, manufacturer web and email address If applicable If applicable If applicable 1j, 1 expected battery lifetime expressed in cycles yes yes If applicable 1j, 2 reference test used for expected battery lifetime expressed in cycles yes yes If applicable 1m period for which the commercial warranty for the calendar life applies If applicable If applicable If applicable 1n, 1 initial round trip energy efficiency yes yes If applicable 1n, 2 round trip energy efficiency at 50 % of cycle-life; yes yes If applicable 1p c-rate of relevant cycle-life test. yes yes If applicable 1q, 2 the marking requirements laid down in Article 13(5) If applicable If applicable If applicable *EV = electrical vehicles LMT = light means of transport IND= industrial batteries

# Page 25^page=25
Data requirements - if applicable Code EV LMT​ IND 4a, 1 Rated capacity (in Ah) yes yes If applicable 4a, 2 capacity fade (in %) yes yes If applicable 4a, 3 Power (in W) yes yes If applicable 4a, 4 power fade (in %) yes yes If applicable 4a, 5 Internal resistance (in Ω) yes yes If applicable 4a, 6 internal resistance increase (in %) yes yes If applicable 4a, 7 Where applicable, energy round trip efficiency (in %) If applicable If applicable If applicable 4a, 8 Where applicable, energy round trip efficiency fade (in %) If applicable If applicable If applicable 4a, 9 The expected life-time of the battery under the reference conditions for which it has been designed, in terms of cycles, except for non-cycle applications yes yes If applicable 4a, 10 The expected life-time of the battery under the reference conditions for which it has been designed, in terms of calendar years yes yes If applicable

# Page 26^page=26
Code EV LMT​ IND 4d, 1 the number of charging and discharging cycles If applicable If applicable If applicable 4d, 2 negative events, such as accidents If applicable If applicable If applicable 4d, 3 periodically recorded information on the operating environmental conditions, including temperature If applicable If applicable If applicable 4d, 4 periodically recorded information on the state of charge If applicable If applicable If applicable Data requirements - if applicable

# Page 27^page=27
Data requirements – not Applicable Code EV LMT​ IND 1c, 1 the carbon footprint declaration NA NA NA 1c, 2 the carbon footprint label NA NA NA 1d information on responsible sourcing as indicated in the report on battery due diligence policy referred to in Article 52(3) NA NA NA 1k capacity threshold for exhaustion (only for electric vehicle batteries); yes NA NA 1t clear, understandable and readable instructions for use in a format that makes it possible to print, download and save them on an electronic device so that the user can access them at all times, in particular during a breakdown of the battery (only for stationary battery energy storage systems NA NA NA 4b, 1 information on the state of health of the battery pursuant to Article 14: state of certified energy (SOCE) yes NA NA

# Page 28^page=28
Data requirements: application varies according to battery category in the same code Code EV LMT​ IND 4b, 2 information on the state of health of the battery pursuant to Article 14: remaining capacity NA yes If applicable 4b, 3 information on the state of health of the battery pursuant to Article 14: where possible, the remaining power capability NA yes If applicable 4b, 4 information on the state of health of the battery pursuant to Article 14: where possible, the remaining round trip efficiency NA yes If applicable 4b, 5 information on the state of health of the battery pursuant to Article 14: [where possible,] the evolution of self-discharging rates NA yes If applicable 4b, 6 information on the state of health of the battery pursuant to Article 14: where possible, the ohmic resistance NA yes If applicable

# Page 29^page=29
Ewout DEURWAARDER Digital Product Passport: Responsibilities and Data5

# Page 30^page=30
Responsibility for the battery passport The responsibility for creating the passport and keeping it up to date lies with the economic operator that places the battery on the EU market, normally: • EU manufacturer, • Importer, or • (in the case of direct sales from outside the EU to the end-user) Operator offering the product for sale May give written authorisation to any other operator to act on its behalf, e.g. service provider, manufacturer (when import), cell manufacturer, OEM, ..

# Page 31^page=31
The manufacturer • The manufacturer is the one that produces/puts together the finished product • Not the same kind of entity in every supply chain • In case where imported components have to be completed to finish the battery, the manufacturer will be in the EU (and there is no importer of the battery) • N.B.(imported) cells and modules have to comply themselves only if they are ‘Do It Yourself’ kits (i.e. made available on the EU market for end use, without any further incorporation or assembly into larger battery packs or batteries)

# Page 32^page=32
The battery • ‘battery’ means any device delivering electrical energy generated by direct conversion of chemical energy, having internal or external storage, and non- rechargeable or rechargeable battery cells, modules or of packs of them, and includes a battery that has been subject to preparation for re-use, preparation for repurposing, repurposing or remanufacturing. • Thus, it is a finished and (safely) working product. For a traction battery this would normally include a battery management system. Similarly, other components that are integral for the(safe) operation of the battery should be considered part of the battery. Thus, if there are such common components, then the whole system consistent of multiple modules or packs and such components should be considered the battery.

# Page 33^page=33
Point of compliance • Passport must exist, filled and registered when battery is placed on EU market (though dynamic data could be largely empty) • For imported batteries: usually when they are presented to EU customs and declared for the release for free circulation procedure

# Page 34^page=34
Further info: Blue Guide

# Page 35^page=35
Data requirements Key issues highlighted in the webinar of 27 May: • Consult projects/standards where needed • Carbon footprint, recycled content and due diligence not yet applicable → • For certain industrial battery categories, some fields are not applicable • State of health /dynamic data: frequency depends on need and proportionality → Where data is not language-neutral: in language or languages which can be easily understood by end-users, as determined by the Member State in which the battery is to be made available on the market Semantic rulebook will be made public

# Page 36^page=36
Carbon footprint, recycled content, due diligence Only in the passports of batteries placed in the EU market after the specific requirement starts to apply (to that category of battery) Only results, no supply chain data. Thus: • Carbon footprint declaration • Carbon footprint label • Recycled content figures • Due diligence public report At present no indication of timing for carbon footprint possible (once adopted, more than a year to comply)

# Page 37^page=37
Frequency of dynamic data At least when change in status of the battery (is considered) (original / repurposed / re-used / remanufactured / waste) More often where proportionate and update is needed for purpose the user of the data is using them for

# Page 38^page=38
Access rights, restricted data, authentication Implementing act on access rights to certain restricted parts (who can access and what they can do with the data) • Stakeholders and Member States consulted on 29 April; feedback received • To be discussed further with Member States • Draft on ‘have your say’ and for WTO/TBT consultation in the coming months Authentication not part of the Commission’s mandate • No centralised database, so no centralized system • Certain national registries could help with identification

# Page 39^page=39
Questions & Answers on specific presentations6

# Page 40^page=40
Break Session will start again soon

# Page 41^page=41
Questions & Answers from previous Webinar7

# Page 42^page=42
Second-life batteries, waste batteries When remanufactured or repurposed, new passport with a new operator taking responsibility • See also Article 45 A repair does not require a new passport, unless it goes as far as being remanufacturing (see definition 32) When becomes waste, responsibility goes to the ‘producer’, the producer responsibilityorganisation or the selected waste management operator Once recycled, the passport shall cease to exist

# Page 43^page=43
(Non)-compliance and enforcement • Market surveillance authorities check, risk-based approach (or on request of customs authorities) • In case of non-compliance, corrective action required • Penalties may be given • Prohibition/restriction/withdrawal/recall (across EU) in case corrective action is not undertaken within a reasonable time • Common challenges are discussed at EU level (‘ADCO group’) • Liability - for defective products - addressed by other legislation

# Page 44^page=44
Closing Remarks & Conclusion8

# Page 45^page=45
Key takeaways and next steps • The datapoints requirements for the battery pass and the technical guidelines for the DPP Registry will be shared on the DPP website, to be launched in the coming days. The website will be hosted within DG GROW website (https://single-market- economy.ec.europa.eu/index_en). • The testing environment of the DPP registry and the associated helpdesk will go live end July. • More technical webinars will follow, exact topics are still under discussion. • Announcements for new policy milestones, new DPP events or webinars will be done in the DG GROW news section

# Page 46^page=46
FAQ published Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)
